hero.sub1: Career-connected learning for opportunity and justice-impacted youth ages 14-17.
hero.sub2: Cybersecurity, entrepreneurship, financial literacy, tech, and creative media — delivered through cohort-based programs with facilitator support.

tracksSection.title: Five Tracks. Your Choice.
tracksSection.sub: Each track builds real skills and connects to real careers.

bands.explorerDesc: Foundation skills, exposure, and discovery. Build confidence with hands-on activities and find what excites you.
bands.launchDesc: Credential prep, portfolio building, and career planning. Get ready for what comes next — college, certification, or career.

outcomes.title: What Outcomes Look Like
outcomes.sub: Each learner leaves the program with concrete artifacts and a next-step plan, not just attendance.
outcomes.portfolioDesc: Capstone security plan, career interest profile, and module completion records — concrete proof of skill.
outcomes.certDesc: Direct on-ramps to Cisco NetAcad, ISC2 Certified in Cybersecurity (CC), and CompTIA Security+.
outcomes.nextStepDesc: Every learner ends with a concrete next action: a cert to pursue, an internship to apply for, or a credential pathway.

delivery.title: Delivery Models
delivery.sub: Built to fit how your program actually runs.
delivery.siteLedDesc: Your staff facilitate using our curriculum, dashboard, and resources. We provide training and support.
delivery.hybridDesc: Shared delivery — your staff lead in-person sessions, we run virtual deep-dives and credential coaching.
delivery.fullServiceDesc: We staff the cohort end-to-end. You provide the space, recruitment, and on-site relationships.

foundations.title: Cyber Foundations

slides.title: What is Cybersecurity?
slides.body: Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting systems, networks, and data from digital attacks. Every device connected to the internet is a potential target — and defending them is a growing global priority.

slides.title: Why It Matters
slides.body: Data breaches cost businesses an average of $4.45 million per incident (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report, 2023). Personal data — passwords, financial info, medical records — is constantly at risk. Critical infrastructure like power grids and hospitals depends on strong cyber defense.

slides.title: Real Breaches
slides.body: In 2017, a single breach exposed the personal data of 147 million people — names, Social Security numbers, birth dates. In 2020, a ransomware attack shut down a major hospital network, diverting emergency patients and delaying care for days. Real incidents like these are why every organization now treats cybersecurity as a business risk, not just an IT problem.

slides.title: Many Ways In
slides.body: You don't need a four-year degree to start a cyber career. People enter the field through bootcamps, free industry certifications (CompTIA Security+, ISC2 Certified in Cybersecurity), military service, IT help desk roles, and self-study with home labs. Employers care about what you can do, not just what's on your diploma.

slides.title: The Opportunity
slides.body: The U.S. had roughly 470,000 unfilled cybersecurity job openings as of mid-2024 (NIST CyberSeek). The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects Information Security Analyst employment to grow about 33% from 2023 to 2033 — much faster than the average for all occupations. The field rewards curiosity, problem-solving, and persistence over any single background.

roles.name: Security Analyst
roles.desc: Monitors networks for threats and investigates alerts

roles.name: Penetration Tester
roles.desc: Legally hacks systems to find vulnerabilities before attackers do

roles.name: Incident Responder
roles.desc: Investigates breaches and leads the recovery effort

roles.name: Security Engineer
roles.desc: Designs and builds security architecture and tools

roles.name: GRC Analyst
roles.desc: Manages governance, risk, and compliance frameworks

roles.name: Digital Forensics
roles.desc: Recovers evidence from devices after breaches and crimes

roles.name: Security Architect
roles.desc: Designs the overall security strategy for an organization

roles.name: CISO
roles.desc: Chief Information Security Officer — leads the entire security program

quiz.q: Why is demand for cyber workers growing?
opts: Digital threats are increasing and there aren't enough defenders

foundations.sourcesDisclaimer: Job-market and salary figures cited above are point-in-time references; verify against current BLS/CyberSeek data before quoting in partner materials.

digitalSafety.title: Digital Safety Sim
digitalSafety.phishingPrompt: Identify the phishing email, then tap the red flags.
digitalSafety.tapFlags: Tap the red flags in this email:
digitalSafety.phishingCorrect: Correct! You spotted the phishing email.
digitalSafety.phishingWrong: Not quite — the first email was the phishing attempt.
digitalSafety.passwordPrompt: Rate each password as weak or strong.
digitalSafety.socialPrompt: An unknown caller says they're from IT support and need your password to fix an issue. What do you do?

socialChoices.verify: Hang up and verify through known channels

digitalSafety.wifiPrompt: On a public Wi-Fi network, which actions are safe?
digitalSafety.breachPrompt: Number these breach-response steps in order from FIRST to LAST.

resources.title: Facilitator Resources
resources.subtitle: Everything you need to run a strong Cyber Launch cohort.

overview.whatIsBody1: Bright Boost Pathways is a career-connected learning program for youth ages 14-17. It delivers hands-on, project-based modules across five tracks: cybersecurity, entrepreneurship, financial literacy, technology, and creative media.
overview.whatIsBody2: The program is designed for delivery in community settings, correctional education, workforce programs, and alternative schools — anywhere young people need real skills and real pathways forward.
overview.explorerDesc: Foundation skills, exposure, and discovery. Build confidence with hands-on activities.
overview.launchDesc: Credential prep, portfolio building, and career planning. Get ready for what comes next.
talkingPoints: Nearly 470,000 cybersecurity jobs are open in the U.S. right now (NIST CyberSeek).
You don't need a 4-year degree to start — free industry certifications like ISC2 Certified in Cybersecurity open real doors.
This program builds real skills students can put on a resume — not just enrichment.
Major employers — IBM, Apple, Amazon — have dropped degree requirements for many tech roles.
overview.newFacilitatorTitle: Where to Start as a New Facilitator
newFacilitatorSteps: Read this Program Overview tab end to end.
Read the Module Guide for Module 1 (Cyber Foundations) — it sets tone for the whole program.
Read the Facilitation Tips tab, especially "Setting Tone Day One."
Print the worksheets you'll need for week 1 and 2 ahead of time.
Create your own Cisco NetAcad account so you can guide students through it.
Have the partner organization's support contacts handy for referrals.

sessions.pacingSub: Pair each week's module with the matching worksheet for stronger outcomes.

moduleGuides.introSub: One full guide per Cyber Launch module. Click a module to expand.
moduleGuides.introBody: Each guide includes what the module teaches, discussion questions for before and after, common sticking points and how to coach through them, real-world context, vocabulary, and red flags to watch for. Read the guide for next week's module BEFORE the session.

faqs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faqs.sub: Real questions from real audiences. Use the answers as starting points — make them your own.

youth.label: From Youth
youth.description: Real questions teenagers ask. Answers facilitators can use word-for-word, or adapt to their voice.

parents.label: From Parents / Guardians
parents.description: Questions guardians ask before letting their teen enroll. Answer them with calm specificity.

partners.label: From Partner Organizations
partners.description: Questions community partners and site leaders ask before signing on to host a cohort.

worksheets.title: Printable Worksheets
worksheets.sub: Each opens in a new tab as a print-ready document. Facilitator notes are visible on screen but hidden in print.
worksheets.body: Worksheets are designed to pair with specific modules. The Session Guide tab shows which worksheet matches each week. Print ahead of session — students who fill them out by hand retain more than those who type.
printTips: Worksheets open in a new tab and auto-trigger the browser print dialog.
If the print dialog doesn't open, press Ctrl/Cmd+P in the new tab.
Facilitator notes (in dashed cyan boxes) are hidden when printing.
For double-sided printing, choose "Long edge" binding in your printer settings.
If you want a clean PDF, choose "Save as PDF" instead of a physical printer.

tips.title: Facilitation Tips
tips.sub: Practical guidance for the situations that arise in real cohorts.
tips.intro: These tips were written by program staff with input from partner facilitators. They are starting points, not strict rules. Adapt to your cohort, your site, and your own style. Sensitive sections (trauma-informed practice, justice-impacted youth) are worth reviewing with your partner organization's clinical or program leadership before applying at scale.
